text: The surgeon lifts the small bowel out of the pelvis, revealing a significant prolapse. The sacral uterine ligaments are visible, indicating that only rectopexy will be performed. The ureter is identified as the peritoneum is opened along the sacral promontory.
The surgeon identifies the left iliac vein, which is positioned close to the surgical field. The peritoneum is incised, and the cul-de-sac is firmly repositioned to reduce the prolapse.
The surgeon dissects along the bare muscular coat of the rectum, contrasting the approach used in rectal cancer cases.
The surgeon creates a tunnel behind the vagina, visible in the surgical field.
The assistant surgeon lifts the vagina, revealing the tunnel created behind it, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon positions a non-resorbable polypropylene mesh strip over the rectum, tailoring its size for optimal coverage. The mesh is used to reinforce the rectal area during the rectopexy procedure.
The surgeon indicates the site of the interception, highlighting the specific area involved in the rectopexy procedure.
The surgeon sutures the anterior aspect of the rectum to the polypropylene mesh, securing it to prevent further interception.
The surgeon displays the finalized suturing of the anterior aspect of the rectum to the mesh, showing the completed attachment within the surgical field.

The surgeon fixes the polypropylene mesh to the sacral promontory without applying undue traction, carefully avoiding the incorporation of the hypogastric nerve. An additional suture is placed at this level to prevent recurrences.
The surgeon places an additional suture to prevent further intussusception higher up, then firmly closes the peritoneal flaps over the mesh, completing the procedure.
The surgeon makes an incision in the distal vagina, cores out the tissue, and closes the vagina on top, following pre-preparation with oestrogel.
